title-group.article-title: Research progress of endophytes and their secondary metabolites in Morus plants

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Morus plants are globally recognized as essential economic woody species. Investigating cross-kingdom interaction mechanisms between Morus and endophytes, along with the regulatory networks of secondary metabolism, is crucial for sustainable agricultural development and innovative drug research. However, systematic analyses remain lacking. The variations in endophytic communities across different mulberry species, varieties, and tissues have not been fully elucidated. This review systematically reviewed the diverse characteristics of endophytes in Morus plants and the biological activities of their secondary metabolites, with particular emphasis on the co-evolution mechanisms between endophytes and their hosts. It was demonstrated that the endophytic communities in Morus plants exhibited significant species-specific differences, tissue specificity, and cultivar dependence. These endophytic communities enhance host nutrient utilization efficiency and promote plant growth through metabolic reciprocity, primarily via nitrogen fixation and phosphorus solubilization. Furthermore, they directly synthesize bioactive metabolites (such as flavonoids and alkaloids) while functioning as biological elicitors that activate host secondary metabolic pathways, thereby facilitating secondary metabolites accumulation. Additionally, endophytes were observed to improve host stress resistance by enhancing photosynthetic efficiency, maintaining ion homeostasis, and regulating soil nutrients. Hairy root cultures facilitate industrial-scale production of secondary metabolites from Morus plants through efficient biosynthesis platforms. This review established a theoretical foundation for in-depth analyses of plant-microbe cross-kingdom interaction networks and the development of targeted regulation technologies. Future research should prioritize investigations into dynamic metabolic interaction mechanisms and ecological safety assessments of engineered strains.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The genus Morus (Moraceae), comprising 16 species globally distributed across temperate/subtropical Eurasia and North America, is an economically important woody plant group.1 China is regarded as this genus's center of diversity and putative origin, hosting 11 wild species and diverse cultivars.2 Key representatives include M. alba L., M. notabilis C. K. Schneid., M. australis Poir., M. cathayana Hemsl., and M. nigra L. These species are widely cultivated for their remarkable adaptability and multifunctional applications.3 They are distributed from northeastern to southwestern China and exhibit significant ecological plasticity, even in extreme environments such as the hyperarid regions of northwestern Xinjiang.

Various parts of the Morus plants possess significant economic value. The mulberry leaves are the exclusive source of feed for Bombyx mori. High-yielding Morus cultivars have been developed by optimizing leaf morphobiochemical traits.4 The root bark (Mori cortex), branches (Mori ramulus), fruits (Mori fructus), and symbiotic fungi (Sanghuangporus) contain abundant secondary metabolites, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids. These compounds, which evolved as a response to defensive stress, exhibit pharmacological activities such as antidiabetic, antioxidant, and antitumor effects.5-8

Recent studies have demonstrated the existence of metabolic crosstalk and coevolution phenomena between endophytes and their host plants.9 Endophytes have been shown to either directly synthesize secondary metabolites identical or similar to those produced by the host plant during its metabolic processes or act as biological elicitors to stimulate the biosynthesis and accumulation of host plant secondary metabolites.10,11 This metabolic synergy is derived from carbon source competition and nutrient mutualism, where the host provides endophytes with photosynthates, and endophytes in return mediate nitrogen fixation and phosphate solubilization.12,13 Such “metabolic bridging” indicates that plant-microbe cross-kingdom interactions represent a key driver of secondary metabolites diversification.

Endophytic diversity in Morus plants exhibits significant organ-specific patterns. However, variation in the microbiome among different species, cultivars, and plant organs remain poorly characterized. Systematic analyzes of the relationship between endophytes and secondary metabolites biosynthesis are scarce, particularly regarding the cross-kingdom regulatory mechanisms underlying plant bioactive compound production. Additionally, the mechanisms of interaction between Morus plants and their endophytes require systematic elucidation (Figure 1).

caption.p: Overview of interaction system between endophytes and Morus plants. Endophytes and Morus plants exhibit co-evolution through metabolic synergy. The Morus plant synthesizes secondary metabolites, whereas endophytes produce structurally and functionally analogous compounds. Acting as bioinducers, endophytes modulate metabolic pathways via signaling molecules, thereby enhancing the production of bioactive compounds. Their symbiotic relationship is primarily based on carbon-nutrient exchange: the mulberry supplies photosynthetic carbon, while endophytes contribute to nitrogen fixation and phosphorus solubilization, thus improving nutrient acquisition. This mutualistic interaction enhances stress tolerance and promotes growth through metabolic regulation, providing significant ecological advantages. Figure created by BioRender.com.

sec.p: Based on the above problems, this study systematically reviewed the research on endophytes in Morus plants, constructed an endophyte community diversity atlas based on metagenomics and culturomics approaches, and categorized endophyte-derived secondary metabolites through bioactivity profiling and mechanistic classification. The study delineated the interaction mechanisms between endophytes and their hosts, revealing the microbiome-driven mechanisms underlying secondary metabolites production in Morus plants. These findings provide a theoretical framework for subsequent investigations in this field.

sec.title: Isolation, identification, and diversity of endophytes in Morus plants
Isolation and characterization of endophytes in Morus plants
sec.p: Endophytes from Morus plants have been successfully isolated from various species, tissues, and organs, primarily using culture-dependent approaches and plate-based isolation techniques. Culture-dependent methods have been extensively utilized owing to their efficiency in screening complex endophytic communities associated with Morus plants, and have established critical foundations for subsequent endophytic diversity analyzes.14 Separately, plate-based isolation techniques are commonly applied to isolate phytopathogen-antagonistic bacteria in Morus plants.15

Endophyte identification methods fall into two main categories: conventional approaches and modern molecular biological techniques. While conventional methods are mainly based on morphological and biochemical analyzes, scanning electron microscopy offers superior resolution for characterizing endophytic microstructures.16 However, these conventional approaches are inherently limited by their requirement for culturable processes. In contrast, modern molecular identification technologies effectively overcome this limitation. Techniques such as internal transcribed spacer (ITS) sequence analysis, ribosomal 16S/18S rRNA gene sequencing, and metagenomic sequencing are widely applied in endophyte identification. Specifically, ITS sequence analysis demonstrates high resolution in species-level identification of endophytes,17 while 18S rRNA gene sequencing, though having a lower taxonomic resolution, retains significant utility in fungal diversity studies. For bacterial endophyte research, 16S rRNA gene sequencing has emerged as the principal molecular tool for analyzing community structure and diversity.18-20

In recent years, the functional capabilities of endophytes has garnered significant research attention. Although conventional 16S/18S rRNA gene sequencing remains valuable for microbial taxonomic classification and evolutionary analysis through specific gene markers, this approach cannot directly elucidate functional capabilities. This limitation is effectively addressed by metagenomic sequencing technology, which enables culture-independent profiling of complete microbial communities through direct extraction and analysis of total community DNA.21 Compared to targeted amplification methods, metagenomic sequencing offers three key advantages: (1) more comprehensive genomic coverage, (2) enhanced detection of microbial diversity, and (3) elimination of primer-related biases.22,23 The integrated application of these techniques provides robust methodological support for investigating the composition, functional attributes, and ecological contributions of endophytes in Morus plants.

In summary, the isolation of endophytes from Morus plants mainly relies on tissue surface disinfection combined with the selective media culture method, supplemented by the plate inhibition method to screen for antagonistic bacteria. Traditional identification has been primarily based on colony morphology and biochemical profiling, whereas modern molecular techniques, particularly ITS sequencing, 16S/18S rRNA gene sequencing, and metagenomic sequencing, have significantly enhanced taxonomic resolution. Metagenomic sequencing facilitates comprehensive functional annotation of microbial communities and reveals the metabolic capabilities of unculturable taxa through culture-independent phylogenetic analysis.

sec.title: Diversity of endophytes in Morus plants
sec.p: Morus plants, as economically important species, harbor diverse endophyte communities that exhibit remarkable species-specific variations (Table 1, Figure 2). Recent studies have identified multiple endophytes from M. nigra L., M. cathayana Hemsl., and M. australis Poir.24-27 However, existing studies remain predominantly focused on M. alba L., whose bacterial endophytic communities are dominated by Bacillus, Pseudomonas, and Streptomyces, characterized by their broad-spectrum metabolic activities and environmental adaptability. The fungal endophytic communities are dominated by Penicillium, Aspergillus, and Trichoderma, which play key roles in secondary metabolites synthesis and biocontrol. Endophyte communities in Morus plants exhibit tissue-specific distribution patterns. Mulberry leaves are predominantly colonized by Bacillus and Pseudomonas, while highly lignified stems harbor Pantoea and Fusarium as dominant genera. Mulberry fruits commonly contain Saccharomyces, lactic acid bacteria, and Acetobacterium associated with sugar metabolism and fruit fermentation. Root endophytes are primarily composed of growth-promoting Rhizobium, Burkholderia, and Paenibacillus, with arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi (AMF) dominating the rhizosphere microbiota. The endophytic communities in Morus plants show cultivar-dependent variation. Resistant cultivars exhibit significantly higher α-diversity of endophytes compared to susceptible counterparts. For instance, Pseudomonas abundance in roots of cold-resistant cultivar X792 reached 46.8% in winter compared to 22.3% in susceptible “Dashi”.28 Similarly, Bacillus abundance in the rhizosphere of resistant Kangqing 10 (13.66%) was 4.03% higher than in susceptible Guisangyou 12 (9.63%), and positively correlated with host peroxidase activity.29 These observations suggest that endophyte communities restructuring contributes to host stress resistance mechanisms.

caption.p: Diversity of endophytes in the Morus plants.

td.italic: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus velezensis, PaeniBacillus polymyxa, Colletotrichum

tr.td: Serratia marcescens, Pseudomonas stutzeri, Bacillus, Rhodococcus

caption.p: Phylogenetic tree of endophytes in Morus plants. Phylogenetic tree constructed using the Neighbor-joining method with 1000 bootstrap replicates, based on gene sequence alignment of endophytes isolated from Morus plants. Bootstrap values reflect clade reliability, illustrating evolutionary relationships among the bacterial strains.

sec.p: The diversity and community structure of endophytes in Morus plants are regulated by environmental factors (such as season and soil stressors) and host genetic characteristics. As a key environmental factor, seasonal variation significantly influences the dynamics of endophytes in Morus plants. The α-diversity of Morus plants' endophytic communities is significantly higher in winter than in autumn and also slightly higher than in spring. This pattern likely results from the enrichment of cold-adapted taxa such as Proteobacteria and Firmicutes.28,37 In spring, the fungal community was dominated by Capnodiales and Pleosporales, whereas autumn communities showed a significant increase in Dothideomycetes relative abundance from 44.17% in spring to 73.13% in autumn.41 These seasonal fluctuations suggest that temperature and climatic conditions shape community structure through selective pressure.

Studies have demonstrated that soil physicochemical properties and abiotic stress intensity regulate both the diversity and functional potential of endophytic communities in Morus plants through environmentally mediated processes. Under salt stress (100 mmol/L NaCl), an increased reliance of Morus plants on Rhizophagus intraradices (RI) was detected (P < 0.05), indicating the critical role of endophytic fungi in ion homeostasis regulation.50 Cd exposure showed dose-dependent effects: low-dose treatment (2 mg/kg) transiently increased bacterial diversity (Shannon index = 3.27 ± 0.19) via activation of stress-resistant taxa such as Pseudomonas spp., whereas high-dose exposure (52 mg/kg) increased the relative abundance of Cd-tolerant phyla (e.g., Firmicutes) but markedly reduced community evenness (Simpson index = 0.0653 ± 0.0013, P < 0.05).52

Endophytic diversity was strongly associated with the health status of Morus plants. In Botrytis-infected mulberry fruits, Cyanobacteria abundance was significantly reduced compared with healthy controls, exhibiting an increased sensitivity to phytopathogens, while Basidiomycota abundance decreased from 3.14% in healthy groups to nearly undetectable levels. Healthy fruits were enriched with pathogen-resistant taxa, including Sclerotiniaceae and Xylariales (linear discriminant analysis score > 3.0), with significantly higher abundance in healthy compared to infected groups (P < 0.05).40 Furthermore, stems of growth-impaired plants were predominantly colonized by Pseudomonas and Pantoea, whereas healthy plants were enriched with Bacillus spp., which significantly promoted mulberry growth through plant-growth-promoting activities (P < 0.05).39

Furthermore, plantation age modulates AMF diversity through alterations in rhizosphere microecology. The hyphal colonization rates of AMF peaked in 2-y-old mulberry, followed by 1-y-old trees, then declined significantly in 3- and 4-y-old plantations.53 This age-dependent pattern correlates with the optimal edaphic conditions in 2-y-old stands: soil pH 6.5, available potassium (120 mg/kg), organic matter (25 g/kg), and peak enzymatic activities (urease: 45 mg/kg.h, sucrase: 60 mg/kg.h), significantly higher than those in other age groups. These dynamics imply that microbial interaction networks are shaped by rhizosphere feedback mechanisms during plant developmental progression.

In summary, endophytic communities in Morus plants have been demonstrated to exhibit significant species differences, site specificity, and variety dependence. Their diversity and structural organization are collectively modulated by external factors (seasonal variation, edaphic stress) and internal factors (host genetic profiles, health status, and plantation age.

sec.title: Study on secondary metabolites and biological activity of endophytes in Morus plants
sec.p: During the coevolution of Morus plants and their endophytes, the latter have developed the ability to produce secondary metabolites (flavonoids, alkaloids, terpenoids, polysaccharides, etc.) that are homologous or structurally similar to those of host plants. These secondary metabolites not only exhibit conventional pharmacological activities such as antioxidant, antitumour, and cytotoxicity, but recent studies have also revealed their significant potential in novel therapeutic applications, including hypoglycemic effects, neuroprotection, and anti-HIV activities (Figure 3).

caption.p: Mechanism figure of antioxidant, antimicrobial, and secondary metabolites accumulation of endophytes and their secondary metabolites in Morus plants. Antioxidant action: The Nrf2/ARE signaling pathway alleviates oxidative stress by regulating the expression of ROS-scavenging enzymes and redox-sensitive signaling molecules. KEAP1 modulates cellular redox homeostasis through Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ interactions. Antimicrobial Activity: Metabolites inhibit pathogen nutrient acquisition and disrupt bacterial cell walls via enzymatic activities such as chitinase and β-1,3-glucanase. Secondary Metabolism Promotion: Endophytes stimulate the synthesis of host phytochemicals and upregulate key enzymes involved in secondary metabolites biosynthetic pathways. Figure created by BioRender.com.

sec.title: Antioxidant substances and their mechanisms of action
Types of antioxidant substances
sec.p: The antioxidant active substances produced by endophytes of Morus plants primarily include anthraquinones, polysaccharides and other compounds, and the strength of their activities is closely related to their chemical structures. Specifically, the quinone-hydroquinone interconversion system in anthraquinones quenches free radicals through reversible electron transfer and is regenerated via redox cycling, the hydroxyl group enhances electron donor capacity, while methyl or methoxy groups prolong antioxidant action, and planar structures facilitate membrane insertion to inhibit lipid peroxidation.54,55 Polysaccharide components, on the other hand, are related to their molecular weight, type of glycosidic bond, spatial structure (helical conformation), and functional group modifications (sulfation, carboxymethylation, etc.).56-58

sec.title: Free radical scavenging mechanisms.
sec.p: The excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species under oxidative stress can lead to oxidative damage to biomolecules. Morus plants endophytes' secondary metabolites scavenge free radicals through two key mechanisms.

Direct scavenging: The endophytic fungus Trametes hirsuta HZ-MB13, isolated from mulberry branches, exhibited potent antioxidant activity with a 53.55% 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radical scavenging rate.59 A DPPH radical scavenging rate of 60% was demonstrated by the γ-pyrone derivative fusarubin, isolated from the mulberry leaves endophyte Fusarium solani JS-0169.33

Synergistic interactions were observed in multicomponent systems in traditional Chinese medicine. The ethyl acetate (EtOAc) extract from mulberry branch endophytic Pseudomonas spp. demonstrated ABTS•+ radical scavenging activity.60 Comparative analysis revealed that the 70% ethanol extract of Sanghuangporus sanghuang exhibited significantly higher total antioxidant capacity compared with aqueous extracts,61 with dose-dependent DPPH radical elimination efficiency.62 Notably, Sanghuangporus sanghuang triterpenoid constituents were identified as potent antioxidant against both DPPH and ABTS•+ radicals,8 whereas the radical scavenging activity of both exopolysaccharides and unsaturated fatty acids shows a concentration-dependent enhancement.63,64
Metal chelation: Botryosphaeria fabicerciana MGN23-3 derived nectriapyrone suppressing Fenton reactions via Fe³⁺ reduction (ferric reducing antioxidant power value = 387 mmol·Trolox/g).27

sec.title: Mechanisms of antioxidant enzyme regulation.
sec.p: Endophytic secondary metabolites maintain intracellular redox homeostasis through four mechanisms: regulating endogenous antioxidant enzymes, targeted free radical scavenging, synergistic cascade reactions, and modulation of gene-metabolic networks. The Sanghuangporus sanghuang exopolysaccharide SHP-2 significantly enhanced serum superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) activities in D-galactose-induced mouse models (P < 0.01).65 Transgenic overexpression of MnCYP710A11 elevated CAT activity to 2.3-fold the level in wild-type mulberry plants.66 Through activation and supplementation of antioxidant enzyme systems, host stress resistance can be significantly enhanced, providing candidate compounds and molecular targets for antioxidant drug development.

sec.title: Antitumour and cytotoxic substances and their mechanisms of action
sec.p: Cancer, which is recognized as a malignant disease posing serious threats to human health and life, has been identified as a major challenge in global public health owing to its highly aggressive nature, susceptibility to recurrence, and marked treatment resistance.67 In antitumour drug discovery and development, cytotoxic activity has been widely regarded as a crucial parameter for evaluating the cancer cell-killing potential of chemical compounds. Drugs with high selectivity and efficient cytotoxicity can destroy cancer cells through specific targeting, significantly enhancing therapeutic efficacy while reducing toxic side effects in normal tissues.68

Cytotoxic activities of mulberry endophytic fungal secondary metabolites were demonstrated across multiple cancer models. Phoma herbarum chloroform fractions from M. nigra L. stems exhibited moderate cytotoxicity against cervical cancer (IC₅₀ = 77.8 mg/mL).24 Furthermore, Botryosphaeria fabicerciana isolates containing mellein and β-orcinaldehyde showed selective cytotoxicity against HepG2/C3A cells (IC₅₀ = 110 μg/mL).27 Three novel polyketides (colletotrichalactones A–Ca) from Colletotrichum sp. JS-0361 displayed varying cytotoxic potencies against MCF-7 cells, with colletotrichalactone A demonstrating the strongest activity (IC₅₀ = 8.98 μg/mL).32 Notably, six polyketides isolated from Colletotrichum queenslandicum EtOAc extracts exhibited moderate antileukemic effects on P-388 cells, with the highest potency being exhibited by garcinia phenylpyranol (IC₅₀ = 37.17 μg/mL).26 The underlying mechanism may be mediated through multiple mechanisms induced by polyketones, including apoptosis induction, tubulin binding, signaling pathway inhibition, and epigenetic modulation.

Additionally, several potential inhibitors of tyrosine kinase, a key regulator of tumor development, have been identified from endophytic fungi. Two isofuranaphthoquinone derivatives, epoxyquinophomopsins A and B derivatives isolated from the fermentation broth of Phomopsis sp., showed remarkable selectivity: epoxyquinophomopsins A exhibited potent inhibitory activity against epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), ErbB-4 receptor tyrosine kinase, and Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) with < 20% remaining activity, whereas epoxyquinophomopsins B specifically targeted BTK. The positive control, erlotinib, completely inhibited EGFR (0% activity).41 Notably, among the macrooxazoles produced by the endophytic fungus Phoma sp. JS0228 in white mulberry, macrooxazole C exhibited potent antiproliferative activity against breast cancer cells and prostate cancer cells.46

The antitumor activity of secondary metabolites from endophytes of Morus plants has transitioned from single activity screening to multi-component and multi-target mechanistic analysis and structure-oriented optimization, which can be followed up by improving the yield of rare compounds through heterologous expression of gene clusters, establishing patient-derived xenograft models to verify in vivo efficacy for preclinical evaluation, and exploring synergistic effects with immune checkpoint inhibitors to further enrich the combined antitumor strategies.

sec.title: Hypoglycemic substances and their mechanism of action
sec.p: Diabetes mellitus, a chronic metabolic disorder with rapidly increasing global prevalence, has seen therapeutic strategies progressively targeting α-glucosidase and α-amylase inhibition. This approach modulates postprandial hyperglycemia by delaying intestinal glucose absorption.69,70 In recent years, endophytes from Morus plants and their secondary metabolites have emerged as a research focus in antidiabetic studies owing to their potent inhibitory effects on carbohydrate-hydrolyzing enzymes. The α-glucosidase inhibitory activity of endophytic fungus Trametes hirsuta HZ-MB13, isolated from mulberry branches, showed a concentration-dependent relationship with extracellular polysaccharide levels.59 Similarly, β-sitosterol and ursolic acid isolated from Alternaria alternata demonstrated dose-dependent inhibition in an in vitro α-amylase inhibition assay,36 while chloroform fractions of Phoma herbarum from M. nigra L. also exhibited comparable inhibitory effects.24 Sanghuangporus sanghuang SY489 EtOAc extract showed a low IC50 value of 1.92 μg/mL against α-glucosidase. Furthermore, the isolated styryl pyranone compounds hypolomine B and hispidin interacted with hydrophobic interactions by inducing the enzyme secondary structure unfolding to form hydrogen bonds to achieve mixed-type inhibition.71 Notably, the inhibitory activity varied across polar fractions of endophytic fermentation broths, with the EtOAc fraction from Pseudomonas viridiflava and Pseudomonas syringae (isolated from mulberry branches) showing the strongest α-glucosidase inhibition (IC50 = 1749 μg/mL).60

sec.title: Antimicrobial substances and their mechanisms of action
sec.p: Endophytes and their secondary metabolites have emerged as pivotal biocontrol resources in sustainable plant disease control, particularly due to their broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties, which function through dual mechanisms: direct antagonism against pathogens and induction of host defense responses.

sec.title: Strain antagonism.
sec.p: The biocontrol potential of endophytes in Morus plants has been systematically evaluated using pathogen-specific antagonism studies. Sclerotinia sclerotiorum, the causal agent of Sclerotinia disease in mulberry, showed significant inhibition (with mycelial growth inhibition reaching 80%) by Actinomycetes isolated through antagonistic screening.72 Trichoderma koningiopsis, isolated from Ciboria shiraiana ascocarps, exhibited significant suppression through nutrient competition-based suppression of both mycelial growth and sclerotia formation in the pathogen.73 Broad-spectrum antifungal efficacy against multiple phytopathogens (Botrytis cinerea, Colletotrichum gloeosporioides, etc.) was exhibited by selected Bacillus strains, with Bacillus XP-27 showing consistent inhibition rates >69% across pathogen groups. Notably, 38% higher control efficacy compared to conventional chemical treatments was achieved in vitro assays.37,74 Pseudomonas aeruginosa YD001 demonstrated particular effectiveness in soil-borne pathogen management, achieving over 70% control efficacy against Ralstonia solanacearum through dual mechanisms of antibiotic production and nutrient competition. This was corroborated by distinct inhibition zones (15 to 30 mm) observed in replicated greenhouse pot trials.43

sec.title: Active secondary metabolites.
sec.p: Significant inhibitory activity against Mycobacterium tuberculosis was demonstrated by five butyrolactones and two orsellinic acid derivatives produced by Aspergillus terreus, with effectiveness also observed against Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus.75 Distinct antifungal activity against Botrytis cinerea, Colletotrichum gloeosporioides, Cercospora beticola, and Rhizoctonia solani was exhibited by 5-methylmellein produced by Morus plants endophytic Alternaria burnsii.76 Selective antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria (Bacillus cereus, Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus subtilis) was displayed by mellein and β-orcinolaldehyde, isolated from M. nigra L. endophytic fungus Botryosphaeria fabicerciana MGN23-3, with a marked reduction in inhibitory potency compared to Gram-negative organisms.27

sec.title: Direct antagonism.
sec.p: A novel strain of Bacillus amyloliquefaciens ZJU1 was isolated from healthy mulberry leaves. This strain showed complete inhibition of pathogenic fungus Botrytis cinerea through secreted antimicrobial peptides, leading to a significant reduction in necrotic areas of infected leaves. The chlorophyll content in treated leaves recovered to levels comparable to healthy controls (P < 0.05).31 In a separate study, the fermentation supernatant of Bacillus sp. K0T24 (isolated from disease-resistant mulberry rhizosphere soil) demonstrated 82.47% growth inhibition against Penicillium chrysogenum. Field applications of this biocontrol agent reduced disease incidence in disease-susceptible mulberry by 55.17%.29

sec.title: Induction of systemic resistance.
sec.p: Botrytis cinerea specifically activates the GH18/GH19 family of chitinase genes to degrade the cell wall of the pathogen (β-1,3-glucanase activity is elevated by 2.1-fold) and inhibit fungal growth. At the same time, the chitin fragments produced by hydrolysis can serve as pathogen-associated molecular patterns that can be specifically recognized by pattern recognition receptors on the host plant cell membrane, activating immune signaling pathways (e.g., MAPK cascade response, Ca2+ signaling oscillations, and ROS bursts), thereby inducing the activation of transcription factors (e.g., WRKY, NPR1) and initiates the expression of defense genes, further enhancing the plant's defense ability.77 Botrytis cinerea specifically activates MnCYP710A11 gene expression (5-fold upregulation, P < 0.001), which enhances cell wall resistance by regulating sterol metabolism,66 and overexpression of MnASIs genes inhibits pathogen α-amylase activity.78 As chemical drugs are prone to cause pathogenic bacteria to develop resistance genes,79 the above findings provide potential targets for the design of novel biocontrol agent, and there is an urgent need to integrate multidisciplinary research approaches to gain a deeper understanding of the pathogenic mechanisms of pathogenic bacteria to develop more efficient biocontrol strategies.80,81

sec.title: Anti-inflammatory effects and their mechanisms
sec.p: Endophytes and their secondary metabolites in Morus plants exhibit diverse anti-inflammatory mechanisms. In macrophages induced by lipopolysaccharide, chloroform and n-hexane fractions from endophytes in M. nigra L. suppressed NO production (inhibition rate over 50%), showing significant anti-inflammatory effects.24 Anthraquinone derivatives isolated from Colletotrichum sp. JS-0367 demonstrated dual modulation of oxidative stress response and inhibition of inflammatory mediators in skin fibroblasts.82 The anti-inflammatory efficacy of Serratia marcescens-derived serrapeptase has been validated in preclinical studies, demonstrating significant alleviation of tissue edema through selective proteolysis of inflammatory fibrin deposits (P < 0.05).46,83

sec.title: Neuroprotective substances and their mechanisms of action
sec.p: Glutamate is recognized as the primary excitatory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system, and its homeostasis imbalance-induced excitotoxicity has been closely associated with stroke, epilepsy, and neurodegenerative disorders.84 Fusarubin, a γ- pyranone derivative from Fusarium solani JS-0169 in mulberry leaves, exhibits neuroprotective activity. In glutamate-induced neuronal injury models, compound-mediated reversal of cellular viability decline was significantly achieved (P < 0.01), and the mechanism was suggested to involve mitochondrial electron carrier and energy metabolism.33

sec.title: Anti-HIV substances and their mechanisms of action
sec.p: New strategies for mitigating the therapeutic challenges posed by HIV hypermutation have emerged from Morus plants and their endophytic secondary metabolites. Four functionally differentiated endophytic strains were isolated from mulberry root bark. MaB01C strain combined broad-spectrum antiviral activity with host cell protection, while MaB02S strain exhibited dose-dependent HIV-1 suppression. MaF01SG strain specifically inhibited viral proteases with an IC50 of 10.64 μg/mL. MaF04C strain was particularly noteworthy, exhibiting nanomolar-level inhibitory activity against critical viral replication markers (β-galactosidase and p24 antigen), which established it as the most therapeutically promising candidate.85

sec.title: Preventive effect of PVR and its mechanism
sec.p: The mechanism of proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) is closely linked to the aberrant activation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway, a pathological process that serves as an important trigger for retinal re-detachment following open-globe injury.86 Previous studies demonstrated that the Diels-Alder-type adduct chalcomoracin (CMR), isolated from fungus-infested mulberry leaves, targets and regulates the pathological behavior of human retinal pigment epithelial cells ARPE-19. Within a safe concentration range (cell survival >90%), AKT phosphorylation was effectively inhibited by CMR (achieving a 78.6% inhibition rate), and p53 expression was significantly reversed (1.8-fold upregulation). Additionally, CMR blocked pathological retinal pigment epithelial cell migration by 37.5% and inhibited collagen contraction by 57.2% (based on area reduction). CMR prevented PVR by dually modulating the AKT/p53 signaling pathway, and its high therapeutic index highlights its clinical therapeutic potential.87

sec.title: Mechanisms of interaction between endophytes of Morus plants and their hosts
Growth promotion
sec.p: Endophytes significantly promoted Morus plants growth and development through multi-pathway metabolic regulation. Bacterial inoculation experiments showed that seedling branch length and root fresh weight were significantly increased by 83.37% and 217.70%, respectively, following Bacillus sp. CW16-5 treatment.37 Similarly, plant height and biomass parameters were significantly enhanced by 31.8%–38.5% after Pseudomonas syringae G2 inoculation.47 Fungal endophytes Talaromyces GS1 and Pseudeurotium GRs12 significantly enhanced stem and root growth parameters by 36.17%–65.83%, collectively demonstrating their growth-promoting potential.51

The mechanisms of plant growth promotion by endophytes focus on four aspects: phytohormone production, siderophore-mediated iron acquisition, phosphorus and potassium solubilization, and biological nitrogen fixation. Plant growth is directly promoted by endophytes through the secretion of hormones such as indole acetic acid (IAA). For instance, the height and biomass of mulberry seedlings were significantly enhanced by Pseudomonas syringae G2.47 In response to insoluble Fe in soil, 90.9% of endophytes in Morus plants were detected to have siderophore-producing capacity, with plant Fe uptake being significantly enhanced by PaeniBacillus polymyxa through Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺ conversion.34,37 Regarding mineral activation, insoluble phosphorus and potassium compounds are solubilized by endophytes via organic acid secretion, exemplified by the phosphorus solubilization effect of Pseudomonas syringae G2 and the potassium-releasing capacity of Klebsiella variicola HGG15.47,88 Additionally, the nitrogen-fixing function was identified in 63.6% of endophytes in Morus plants, with soil nitrogen effectiveness significantly enhanced by Pantoea sp. CA15-44 through nitrogen-fixing enzyme activity.37

sec.title: Salt stress response
sec.p: Key regulatory roles in salt stress adaptation in Morus plants are played by AMF. The stress tolerance of plants was enhanced through multiple mechanisms by the dominant strains Funneliformis mosseae (FM) and RI. Specifically, RI inoculation significantly increased mulberry biomass (leaf and stem fresh weight increased by 102%–117%) and photosynthetic pigment content (P < 0.01), while simultaneously reducing leaf malondialdehyde concentration by 36%. This treatment also increased antioxidant enzyme activity by 15%–30% and effectively alleviated salt stress damage.50 FM treatment significantly promoted seedling photosynthetic efficiency (net photosynthetic rate was increased by 99.85%) and enhanced carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus accumulation, indicating improved nutrient uptake promotes growth.89 Sex-specific response studies revealed that proline, micronutrient uptake, and root-to-shoot ratio were increased by female plants to adapt to salt stress, whereas male plants maintained stem ion homeostasis through elevated K⁺/Na⁺ ratio regulation. Under 200 mmol/L NaCl stress, the dry weight reduction of female plants (18.4%) was found to be significantly lower than that of male plants (51.4%), thereby verifying the effectiveness of the sex-differentiated salt tolerance strategies (P < 0.05).90,91

sec.title: Heavy metal repair
sec.p: AMF promote targeted lead translocation to high-light-exposed mulberry by regulating the expression of MaABCG16 and MaABCG19 genes, enhancing remediation efficiency by 2.3-fold.92,93 In a separate study, Rhodococcus sp. MAPN-1, isolated from the rhizosphere soil of white mulberry was shown to degrade polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), with inoculated plants exhibiting a 5-fold increase in PCB removal from 300 mg/kg contaminated soil (P < 0.01).48

sec.title: Carbon and nitrogen cycle regulation
sec.p: Inoculation of FM significantly increased the leaf C:N ratio (14.51%) and enhanced stress tolerance by stabilizing C:N homeostasis (P < 0.05).94

AMF improve photosynthetic efficiency, maintain ionic homeostasis, regulate regulate soil nutrients, improve mulberry resilience through these mechanisms, provide a theoretical foundation for mulberry cultivation and improvement in salinized, rocky desertified, and polluted soils.

sec.title: Regulation of secondary metabolites
sec.p: Plant secondary metabolic pathways are activated by elicitors through their specific recognition of plasma membrane-localized pattern recognition receptors. This recognition triggers a series of early signaling events, including MAPK cascade activation, Ca²⁺ flux oscillations, and ROS burst, which collectively regulate the expression of secondary metabolism-associated genes95 (Figure 3). Elicitors are primarily classified into two categories based on their diverse origins: biological elicitors (e.g., fungal polysaccharides, bacterial flagellins) and abiotic elicitors (e.g., UV radiation, heavy metal ions).96 Biological elicitors have been extensively investigated in metabolic engineering due to their target specificity and high signal transduction efficiency.

Recent studies have revealed the regulatory network of fungal polysaccharide elicitors on the synthesis of triterpenes in Sanghuangporus sanghuang. Following 7 d treatment with 50 μg/mL Poria tenuis mycelial polysaccharide elicitor, NO levels in Sanghuangporus sanghuang were elevated approximately 2.5-fold compared to untreated controls, displaying a triphasic kinetic profile characterized by an initial increase, followed by a marked decline, and subsequent resurgence. Transcriptomic analysis revealed marked upregulation of HMGCR genes, driving enhanced production of mevalonic acid (MVA) as the central biosynthetic intermediate. Concurrently, elicitation promoted the synthesis of key terpenoid precursors, farnesyl pyrophosphate and geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate. Total triterpenoid yields demonstrated a 37.11% increase compared to untreated controls, with specific components 2-hydroxyoleanolic acid and betulinic acid exhibiting 114.67-fold and 2.62-fold enhancements.97

Yeast extract (YE) is widely utilized as a fungal elicitor in plant tissue culture systems. Treatment with 2 mg/mL YE for 72 h, intracellular accumulation of oxyresveratrol (OXY) was observed to increase by 2.1-fold compared to the control group. After 24 h of elicitation, the mulberroside A (MuA) content was significantly increased to 22.0 mg/g DW (dry weight), representing a 1.5-fold increase compared to the control.98,99 A marked synergistic effect was demonstrated upon co-treatment with YE and methyl jasmonate (MJ). Combined treatment with 0.05 mmol/L MJ and 0.5 mg/mL YE for 24 h, the total MuA production represented a 2.3-fold increase compared to control levels. Prolonged treatment to 72 h induced 3.8-fold and 1.9-fold increases in OXY and resveratrol (RES) accumulation, respectively, compared to untreated samples.98 At higher concentrations (YE 2 mg/mL + MJ 0.2 mmol/L), three secondary metabolites showed enhanced production, with RES demonstrating the smallest increment.99 The co-treatment of YE and MJ significantly enhanced the elicitation effect compared to individual applications. This synergistic interaction was attributed to the coordinated activation of phenylpropanoid pathway enzymes (e.g., phenylalanine ammonia-lyase) by YE and the concurrent induction of stilbene synthase expression by MJ, thereby confirming the pivotal regulatory function of synergistic mechanisms in secondary metabolic pathways.

The above multi-target regulatory mechanisms suggest that the synergistic activation of the MVA pathway occurs through the NO-Ca²⁺-MAPK signaling axis by fungal polysaccharide elicitors, whereas YE and MJ regulate key enzymes in phenylpropanoid metabolism. This interplay provides a novel strategy for the directed biosynthesis of secondary metabolites.

sec.title: Scale production of secondary metabolites based on trichome root culture system
Hairy root induction mechanism and genetic transformation system
sec.p: The industrial production of secondary metabolites from Morus plants is constrained by environmental sensitivity and high separation costs, whereas Agrobacterium rhizogenes-mediated hairy root systems provide a breakthrough solution. The molecular mechanism underlying hairy root induction is primarily mediated through T-DNA transfer and subsequent genomic integration. Initially, the vir region genes carried by the Ri plasmid are activated in Agrobacterium, followed by recognition and cleavage of the T-DNA border sequences (LB/RB). The resulting single-stranded DNA-protein complex is assembled and subsequently translocated into wounded plant cells through the type Ⅳ secretion system.100 The T-DNA fragment is stably incorporated into the host plant's nuclear genome, wherein the rolB gene product upregulates endogenous IAA levels through suppression of IAA-conjugating β-glucosidase activity,101 while the rolC gene product facilitates accumulation of zeatin riboside-type cytokinins via enhanced hydrolysis of cytokinin glycosides. These synergistic actions collectively induce root primordium differentiation, ultimately leading to the development of transgenic roots displaying characteristic hairy root morphology, including abolished geotropic response, significantly enhanced lateral root formation, and hormone-independent growth102 (Figure 4).

caption.p: Systematic figure of hairy roots induced by endophytes in Morus plants. Agrobacterium rhizogenes activates Ri plasmid vir genes, transferring T-DNA (via type IV secretion system) as a single-stranded DNA-protein complex into wounded plant cells. Integrated T-DNA in the host genome expresses rolB and rolC, synergistically inducing root primordia differentiation into hairy roots. This system serves as a platform for improving secondary metabolites production, optimized via elicitors, media adjustments, and bioreactor-scale strategies. Figure created by BioRender.com.

sec.title: Strain-specific metabolic regulation
sec.p: Significant structural variations in T-DNA among Agrobacterium rhizogenes strains profoundly regulate secondary metabolites production. Plasmid pRi1724 was harbored by LBA9402 strain, which demonstrated a transformation efficiency of 92.7% in hairy root induction, with induced betulin (5.44 ± 0.41 mg/g DW) and betulinic acid (2.39 ± 0.15 mg/g DW) yields being significantly higher than those of other strains (P < 0.05). The pRiA4 plasmid was carried by R1000 strain, which exhibited relatively high biomass accumulation (153 ± 12.6 mg/flask), although still lower than that of LBA9402 strain (175 ± 8.7 mg/flask). The weakest secondary metabolites production was observed in R1601 strain, attributable to a deletion in the ORF13–14 segment of its T-DNA region.103

sec.title: Process optimization and production enhancement strategies
sec.p: A significant enhancement in target metabolite yields was achieved through a multimodal induction strategy. White mulberry hairy roots were treated with a combined elicitor solution consisting of 13.8 mmol/L methyl-β-cyclodextrin, 3 mmol/L H₂O₂, and 1 mmol/L MgCl₂ over 96 h. Hydroxyresveratrol accumulation was recorded at levels exceeding 6.27 mg/g DW, representing a 13.93-fold increase compared to the control group (industrial threshold: 5 mg/g DW). After 196 h of treatment, moracin M production was detected at 7.82 mg/g DW. In addition, the continuous and efficient production of secondary metabolites can be achieved through the establishment of a scalable cultivation system (such as an integrated flask-bioreactor system), supplemented with periodic elicitation and periodic medium replenishment.104

The hairy root system has emerged as a pivotal platform in plant synthetic biology due to its rapid biomass accumulation, genetic stability, and metabolic versatility. Targeted compound production can be substantially augmented through optimized elicitor combinations and bioreactor configurations, enabling scalable industrial production. Three strategic directions should be emphasized: (1) establishment of CRISPR-dCas9-regulated multigene metabolic pathways, (2) implementation of precision metabolic engineering via multi-omics integration, and (3) development of bioreactors with microcarrier systems. These advancements are expected to maximize the biotechnological potential of hairy root systems for translational applications in endophyte engineering of Morus plants and targeted compound biosynthesis.

sec.title: Summary and outlook
sec.p: The endophytic communities in Morus plants demonstrate significant species variation, tissue specificity, and cultivar dependency, which are regulated by multidimensional regulatory factors, including external environmental conditions (seasonal variations and soil stressors) and host-associated characteristics (genetic traits, health status, and plantation age). Contemporary investigations of bioactive secondary metabolites from these endophytes have transitioned from single-activity screening to multi-component structure-guided optimization. Simultaneously, mechanistic studies have advanced from single-target analyzes to systematic characterization of multi-target synergistic interactions and coordinated multi-mechanism pathways.

Nutrient competition and metabolic reciprocity synergistically interact in Morus plants and endophytes via plant-microbe networks. Enhanced nutrient acquisition efficiency and accumulation in host plants are facilitated through endophytic mechanisms, including plant hormone modulation, mineral solubilization, and biological nitrogen fixation. Concomitantly, stress tolerance is enhanced through photosynthetic efficiency optimization, ionic homeostasis maintenance, and soil nutrient regulation. Furthermore, targeted biosynthesis of defensive secondary metabolites in host plants is elicited by endophyte-derived biological elicitors. Current advancements utilizing hairy root-bioreactor systems demonstrate substantial improvements in targeted compound yields, indicating considerable translational potential for industrial applications.

Current research on endophytes in Morus plants reveals concomitant challenges and opportunities. The spatiotemporal dynamics governing host-microbe metabolic interactions lack systematic characterization, particularly regarding circadian rhythm-driven metabolic fluctuations and their underlying molecular regulatory networks. These limitations may be mitigated through multi-omics integration strategies, where spatiotemporal transcriptomic databases require establishment to identify critical regulatory nodes. Simultaneously, ecological risks associated with synthetic biology-modified endophytes necessitate comprehensive assessment, potentially via the development of CRISPR-dCas9 regulatory modules for constructing synthetic microbial consortia. Furthermore, evolutionary conservation and species-specificity mechanisms in cross-species metabolic networks await elucidation, wherein nanocarrier-mediated delivery systems could be employed to enhance the efficiency of intercellular metabolite translocation.

In conclusion, a highly coordinated symbiotic network between Morus plants and their endophytes has evolved, the mechanistic elucidation of which requires integrating multidisciplinary approaches encompassing microbial ecology, synthetic biology, and systems biology. Future research endeavors should focus on three critical dimensions: spatial and temporal patterns of metabolic interactions, ecological safety assessments of engineered microbial strains, and synergistic biosynthesis mechanisms of plant-specific secondary metabolites. These research priorities are expected to establish a theoretical foundation for developing novel plant-microbe integrated systems with optimized biotechnological potential.

sec.p: Yiwei Geng: Writing – review and editing, Writing – original draft. Wei Geng: Writing – review and editing. Chao Song: Writing – review and editing. Yuqing Tian: Writing – review and editing. Liang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review and editing. Wei Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review and editing, Funding acquisition.
